name: Daksha
description: Daksha is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, often depicted as a Prajapati, a progenitor of humankind. He is considered one of the sons of Brahma, born from his right thumb. Daksha is renowned for his role in the creation of the universe and is seen as a powerful king and a skilled administrator. He is also known for his grand yajnas (sacrifices). A significant event associated with Daksha is the Daksha Yajna, where he insulted his daughter Sati and her husband, Shiva. This act led to Sati's self-immolation, triggering Shiva's wrath and the destruction of Daksha's Yajna. Daksha was later beheaded by Virabhadra, a fierce warrior created by Shiva, but was subsequently revived with a goat's head as a replacement. His lineage is vast, with numerous daughters who married various deities and sages, contributing to the expansion of the universe's population. He embodies the principles of dharma (righteousness) and plays a crucial role in the cosmic order. Despite his flaws, particularly his pride and animosity towards Shiva, Daksha remains a central figure in Hindu cosmology and genealogical narratives. He is often invoked during ceremonies and sacrifices, and his story serves as a cautionary tale about the consequences of arrogance and disrespect.
subtitle: Progenitor and Lord of Creation

keyMyths.title: Daksha Yajna
keyMyths.description: Daksha organized a grand sacrifice but intentionally excluded Shiva. His daughter Sati, Shiva's consort, felt deeply insulted and immolated herself in the sacrificial fire.

keyMyths.title: Birth of Daksha
keyMyths.description: Daksha was born from the right thumb of Brahma, the creator god. He was tasked with the responsibility of populating the universe.

keyMyths.title: Revival with Goat Head
keyMyths.description: After being beheaded by Virabhadra, Shiva's avatar, Daksha was revived by Shiva. He was given a goat's head as a sign of repentance and mercy.

sources.author: Vayu
sources.description: Details Daksha's lineage and role in creation.
